text: sharp burst of gunfire forty meters distant. The clatter tumbles through the open window like soft rain. Answering fire further off. Silence
Baghdad needs assessment. There are so many. Our partners ask for training centers, water, electricity, and shopping. Yes, shopping centers.

A slight haze obscures 
the morning sun, its rays pierce 
the fog and turn pastel 
reflecting dust and city life

two birds, playfighting
on the roof of a building

helicopters in 
the distance, seem to float, with
out effort, on the viscous morning
two shots fired, close. warning shots from perimeter security? snipers? a bored, unemployed Iraqi with nothing better to do?
the two shots fired yesterday? two Iraqi policeman, gone. My boss and I look for a place to drink a beer and look out at the city. We wonder
committee meeting within the Baghdad provincial council. Iraq's democracy is vibrant!! But, more soldiers and diplomats attended than Iraqis
a bright summer morning, blessedly free from the din of generators. a chance to go shooting, but miles of reports to go before I sleep
five heavy gunshots. more distant fire later. siren wails ascend and descend. like every day, black smoke rises behind a school
black cats no good, the guard informed me. This one a recent mother of four and hasn't eaten in two days. I give her tuna bought at the PX
slept in this morning. the bird chatter pleasantly loud, but now the generators are up and drowning it out. the black cat moved her children
helicopter roars overhead, close enough to set off car alarms on the ground. Tomorrow depart on my R&R. One more cup of coffee, then I'll go
returned after some rest outside. after a month here, the mind turns to cheese. our soldiers put up with far worse. they are everday heroes
Indirect fire at 200 meters; black smoke rises behind a building. in front of the building, traffic flows as though not much had happened.
invited inside the home of a Shia-Kurd family. The head of household informs me that Baathists are still killing people, and Maliki is good.
bomb blast at several hundred meters, deep, an external heartbeat. cries of exclamation below, helicopter and falling chaff above, clatter.
I am sure it is coming, my language tutor informs, referring to normalcy in Iraq. Not necessary to knowing the time, but it must coming.
Iraqis explaining what happens to them have a constant manner: the lower lip quivers, eyes blinded by sorrow and rage. not far from despair
Storing C4 in vehicle fuel cylinders is a new method, I am told. A 7-year old girl opened a fuel cap at a gas station, and was badly burned
A morning tinted orange, a symptom of dust storms. I have seen storms that blot out the sun and make the building smell of desert. Not today
Three working days left, then I prepare for home. I feel I might have another year of service left in me, but for now it is time to go home
Baghdad service center stripped and perhaps forgotten already. Leave midJune to reconstruct my life. Ready for home, but I will miss Iraq so
Journalist killed in Amarah. Since 2005, three former team members of a colleague have now died by homicide. The assassinations just go on.
a young bird perched on an electrical wire. dog barking urgently in the distance, as though trying to warn someone. The city seems so quiet
Graham Greene's daily quota was 350 words. My less illustrious output is 140 characters. And I am still 26 short.
the end comes, this lifeless singsong of futility, squawking furiously at water's edge for chips from sucker passersby. don't feed the beast
Clearing out my office. In the notes of a Iong ago workshop on performance plans, read: DURKHEIM, WEBER NOT HELPFUL. What does that mean?
the stir of life in the mornings-the beeps of electrical devices winking on and off, the shouts of armed men, the generators' kick and roar
I hereby predict that the Kurdish capital of Erbil -- perhaps in partnership with Baghdad and/or Sulaimaniyah -- will host the 2040 Olympics
Water spurts from rusty spigots and pools, unabsorbed, on the stiff earth. The gardener claps and announces: "Today water is coming!"
visiting Baghdad after 17 months. a calmed city. T-walls and bridge columns along route irish painted purple, mauve, yellow, red, and black
a gentle storm settles the dust permeating the last three days-the grains covered our floors; wedged in keyboards; turned our inhales chalky
two Christian families attacked last night in south Kirkuk. Three women dead in one home, one brother dead another hospitalized in the other
RT @ellievhall: I had to do it. The Nunes face and expression demanded it. https://t.co/ypULFzmGWQ
@MoritzPoll Si Trangucci Gabry Gelman (2020) "Bayesian hierarchical weighting adjustment and survey inference"
@andrewheiss tho it seems the subsections don't collapse under their respective sections
@cremieuxrecueil As in, fully intact? Fremen are Zensunni, Tleilax are Zensufi, and Orange Catholic Bible still common
@iamnot_elon I worry that will happen. Ending catch and release and deporting criminals should be sufficient.
